channel.title: Services | RemoteWebAdmin on Remote Web Admin | Managed OpenClaw AI Assistant Service
channel.description: Recent content in Services | RemoteWebAdmin on Remote Web Admin | Managed OpenClaw AI Assistant Service

item.title: OpenClaw Personal Installation | RemoteWebAdmin
item.description:
OpenClaw personal installation puts a private, self-hosted AI assistant on your messaging app of choice - today, not after a weekend of troubleshooting.

OpenClaw is open-source software that connects WhatsApp, Telegram, Discord, and iMessage to AI models including Claude, GPT-4, and Gemini. It runs on a small VPS that costs around $5 per month. The data never leaves your server. There are no per-message fees. You own the entire stack.

item.title: OpenClaw Business Installation | RemoteWebAdmin
item.description:
OpenClaw business installation replaces a fragmented mix of individual AI subscriptions with a single, self-hosted platform your entire team shares - deployed in days, not sprints.

When teams grow, AI tool sprawl follows. One person uses ChatGPT, another uses Claude, a third hasn’t started yet. There’s no shared context, no consistent behavior, and a per-seat bill that grows every time someone joins. ChatGPT Team runs $25 per user per month - for a team of 20, that’s $500 a month, every month, with your business conversations processed on a third-party server.

item.title: Messaging Channel Setup | RemoteWebAdmin
item.description:
Messaging channel setup connects your OpenClaw AI assistant to the apps you and your team already use every day - so AI becomes part of your natural workflow, not a separate tool you have to remember to open.

OpenClaw supports WhatsApp, Telegram, Discord, iMessage, and Mattermost out of the box. Each channel has its own API requirements, registration flows, and credential formats. WhatsApp requires Meta Business verification. iMessage requires an Apple Push Notification Service certificate on a macOS host. Discord requires an OAuth2 app with the right permission scopes. None of these are obvious, and one misconfiguration silently breaks everything.

item.title: Maintenance & Monitoring | RemoteWebAdmin
item.description:
Maintenance and monitoring is the difference between an AI assistant that runs reliably for years and one that quietly goes offline every few weeks until someone notices.

OpenClaw runs on a stack of components - Node.js, nginx, PM2, Let’s Encrypt certificates, and the OpenClaw process itself. Each of these can fail independently, and most failures are silent. PM2 may restart a crashed process successfully but log the error nowhere visible. An SSL certificate expires at midnight. A Node.js patch changes a dependency that OpenClaw relies on. Without active monitoring and a regular maintenance cadence, small problems accumulate until something breaks hard.

item.title: AI Feature Add-ons | RemoteWebAdmin
item.description:
AI feature add-ons transform your OpenClaw installation from a conversation interface into an AI assistant that can actually do things - search the web, manage your calendar, read your emails, and run custom workflows on your behalf.

A freshly installed OpenClaw instance is a capable conversational AI connected to your messaging apps. That alone is valuable. But the real power comes from giving your assistant tools: the ability to look something up, schedule a meeting, draft and send an email, or trigger a webhook in response to a message. This is what separates a useful assistant from a novelty.

item.title: Upgrades & Migrations | RemoteWebAdmin
item.description:
OpenClaw upgrades and server migrations keep your AI assistant current, fast, and running on the infrastructure that makes the most sense - without the risk of breaking something that’s working.

Upgrading a live OpenClaw instance is not complicated when you know what you’re doing. The challenge is knowing what you’re doing. Node.js major versions require explicit migration steps. OpenClaw releases sometimes rename configuration keys or restructure plugin interfaces. Running npm update without reviewing the changelog first is how a working assistant becomes a broken one at midnight.
